annotations.label: Exponentiation (positive integer exponent)

anchor.snippet: When n is a positive integer , exponentiation corresponds to repeated multiplication of the base

annotations.note: `Monoid.npow` is the canonical inductive definition of iterated multiplication on a monoid.

anchors.snippet: When n is a positive integer , exponentiation corresponds to repeated multiplication of the base

annotations.label: Multiplication rule for exponents

anchor.snippet: the multiplication rule

annotations.note: `pow_add` proves `a^(m+n) = a^m * a^n` in any monoid.

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}b^{n}\times b^{m}&=\underbrace {b\times \dots \times b} _{n{\text{ times}}}\times \underbrace {b\times \dots \times b} _{m{\text{ times}}}\\[1ex]&=\underbrace {b\times \dots \times b} _{n+m{\text{ times}}}=b^{n+m}.\end{aligned}}}

annotations.label: Zeroth, negative, and fractional powers

anchor.snippet: Exponentiation can also be extended to powers that are not positive integers

annotations.note: Integer powers via `zpowRec`/`DivInvMonoid.zpow`, and rational/real extensions via `Real.rpow`.

anchors.snippet: Exponentiation can also be extended to powers that are not positive integers

annotations.label: Real exponents (extension)

anchor.snippet: The definition of exponentiation can be extended in a natural way

annotations.note: `Real.rpow` defines exponentiation for real exponents.

annotations.label: Archimedes' law of exponents for powers of ten

anchor.snippet: Archimedes proved the law of exponents

annotations.note: Mathlib has the general `pow_add` law; the Archimedes historical specialization to powers of ten is not stated separately.

annotations.label: Square of b

anchor.snippet: is called "the square of b " or " b squared"

annotations.note: `sq a = a * a = a^2` is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cube of b

anchor.snippet: is called "the cube of b " or " b cubed"

annotations.note: There is no dedicated `cube` decl; one uses `a^3` directly.

annotations.label: 3 to the 5th power

anchor.snippet: The base 3 appears 5 times in the multiplication

annotations.note: Specific numeric example; no dedicated lemma.

annotations.label: Inductive definition of positive integer exponentiation

anchor.snippet: The definition of the exponentiation as an iterated multiplication can be formalized by using induction

annotations.note: `Monoid.npow_zero` and `Monoid.npow_succ` provide the inductive base and step.

annotations.label: Associativity gives exponent product rule

anchor.snippet: The associativity of multiplication implies that for any positive integers m and n

annotations.note: `pow_add` formalizes `a^(m+n) = a^m * a^n` from monoid associativity.

annotations.label: Zero exponent

anchor.snippet: a (nonzero) number raised to the 0 power is 1

annotations.note: `pow_zero a : a^0 = 1` is proved in any monoid.

annotations.label: Zero to the power of zero

anchor.snippet: The case of 0 0 is controversial

annotations.note: Mathlib follows the convention `0^0 = 1` via `pow_zero`.

annotations.label: Negative integer exponents

anchor.snippet: Exponentiation with negative exponents is defined by the following identity

annotations.note: `zpow_neg` gives `a^(-n) = (a^n)⁻¹`.

annotations.label: Negative exponents in a monoid

anchor.snippet: The same definition applies to invertible elements in a multiplicative monoid

annotations.note: `DivInvMonoid.zpow` defines integer powers for invertible elements.

annotations.label: Exponent rules (integer exponents)

anchor.snippet: often called exponent rules , hold for all integer exponents

annotations.note: `zpow_add`, `zpow_mul`, `mul_zpow` cover the integer exponent rules.

annotations.label: Non-commutativity and non-associativity of exponentiation

anchor.snippet: exponentiation is not commutative

annotations.note: Mathlib does not state non-commutativity/associativity of `^` as theorems.

annotations.label: Binomial formula

anchor.snippet: The powers of a sum can normally be computed from the powers of the summands by the binomial formula

annotations.note: `Commute.add_pow` is the binomial theorem for commuting elements.

annotations.label: Combinatorial interpretation of n^m

anchor.snippet: the value of n m is the number of functions from a set of m elements to a set of n elements

annotations.note: `Fintype.card_fun` gives `card (α → β) = card β ^ card α`.

annotations.label: Powers of ten in decimal

anchor.snippet: integer powers of 10 are written as the digit 1 followed or preceded by a number of zeroes

annotations.note: Notational observation; no Mathlib lemma states it.

annotations.label: Cardinality of the power set is 2^n

anchor.snippet: a set with n members has a power set

annotations.note: `Fintype.card_finset` proves `card (Finset α) = 2 ^ card α`.

annotations.label: Every power of one is one

anchor.snippet: Every power of one equals

annotations.note: `one_pow n : (1:M)^n = 1`.

annotations.label: Powers of zero

anchor.snippet: For a positive exponent n > 0 , the n th power of zero is zero

annotations.note: `zero_pow` proves `(0:M₀)^n = 0` for `n ≠ 0`.

annotations.label: Alternating sign of powers of -1

anchor.snippet: Since a negative number times another negative is positive

annotations.note: `neg_one_pow_eq_pow_mod_two` captures the alternation; related `Odd.neg_one_pow` etc.

annotations.label: Divergence of powers when b>1

anchor.snippet: The limit of a sequence of powers of a number greater than one diverges

annotations.note: `tendsto_pow_atTop_atTop_of_one_lt` proves `b^n → ∞` when `1 < b`.

annotations.label: Powers with |b|<1 tend to zero

anchor.snippet: Powers of a number with absolute value less than one tend to zero

annotations.note: `tendsto_pow_atTop_nhds_zero_of_lt_one` proves the limit-to-zero result.

annotations.label: Powers of negative bases do not converge

anchor.snippet: alternate between positive and negative as n alternates between even and odd

annotations.note: No dedicated non-convergence lemma for `b<-1` in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Power functions

anchor.snippet: are sometimes called power functions

annotations.note: The terminology `power function` has no dedicated Mathlib decl.

annotations.label: Even and odd power functions

anchor.snippet: two primary families exist

annotations.note: No `evenPowerFunction`/`oddPowerFunction` family is defined.

annotations.label: n th root of a nonnegative real

anchor.snippet: denotes the unique nonnegative real n th root of x

annotations.note: Nth roots are realized as `x ^ (1/n : ℝ)` using `Real.rpow`.

annotations.label: Rational exponent of a positive real

anchor.snippet: If x is a positive real number, and

annotations.note: `Real.rpow` covers rational (and real) exponents on positive reals.

annotations.label: Zero to a positive rational power

anchor.snippet: If r is a positive rational number

annotations.note: `Real.zero_rpow` proves `(0:ℝ)^r = 0` for `r ≠ 0`.

annotations.label: Failure of rational exponent identity for negative base

anchor.snippet: a negative real number has a real n th root

annotations.note: Specific paradox example; no dedicated theorem.

annotations.label: Real exponents via continuity or logarithm

anchor.snippet: exponentiation to real powers can be defined in two equivalent ways

annotations.note: `Real.rpow_def_of_pos` gives `x^y = exp (log x * y)` for `x > 0`.

annotations.label: Principal value and multivaluedness for negative bases

anchor.snippet: exponentiation to a real power of a negative real number is much more difficult to define consistently

annotations.note: `Real.rpow_def_of_neg` gives a specific principal-value formula for `x < 0` but does not develop the multivalued discussion.

annotations.label: Real exponent via limit of rational exponents

anchor.snippet: exponentiation of a positive real number b with an arbitrary real exponent x can be defined by continuity

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Real.rpow` via `exp ∘ log` rather than as a limit of rational approximations, though continuity is proved.

annotations.label: b^π via rational approximations

anchor.snippet: the non-terminating decimal representation

annotations.note: Particular example; no dedicated lemma.

annotations.label: Exponential function (limit definition)

anchor.snippet: There are many equivalent ways to define the exponential function

annotations.note: `Real.exp` is defined via the power series; the `(1+x/n)^n` limit form is not the primary definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponential identity

anchor.snippet: the exponential identity (or multiplication rule)

annotations.note: `Real.exp_add` proves `exp (x+y) = exp x * exp y`.

annotations.label: Euler's number

anchor.snippet: Euler's number can be defined as

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `Real.exp 1` for Euler's number; `Real.exp_one_pos` etc.

annotations.label: Extension of exp to complex arguments

anchor.snippet: The limit that defines the exponential function converges for every complex value of x

annotations.note: `Complex.exp` is the complex exponential.

annotations.label: Real exponentiation via logarithm

anchor.snippet: the natural logarithm ln( x ) is the inverse of the exponential function

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `x^y = exp (log x * y)` for positive `x` via `Real.rpow_def_of_pos`.

annotations.label: Complex exponent with positive real base

anchor.section: Complex exponents with a positive real base
anchor.snippet: exponentiation with base b and complex exponent z is defined by means of the exponential function

annotations.note: `Complex.cpow_def_of_ne_zero` realises `b^z = exp (log b * z)`.

annotations.label: Euler's formula

anchor.section: Complex exponents with a positive real base
anchor.snippet: Euler's formula

annotations.note: `Complex.exp_mul_I` is Euler's formula `exp (x*I) = cos x + sin x * I`.

annotations.label: Polar form of a complex number

anchor.section: n th roots of a complex number
anchor.snippet: Every nonzero complex number z may be written in polar form

annotations.note: `Complex.abs_mul_exp_arg_mul_I` writes `z = ‖z‖ * exp(arg z * I)`.

annotations.label: Polar form of n th roots of a complex number

anchor.section: n th roots of a complex number
anchor.snippet: the polar form of an n th root of a complex number can be obtained by taking the n th root of the absolute value and dividing its argument by n

annotations.note: Mathlib has nth-root theory and `Complex.cpow` but no single decl in polar form.

annotations.label: n distinct n th roots of a complex number

anchor.section: n th roots of a complex number
anchor.snippet: provides the n n th roots of the complex number

annotations.note: `Complex.card_nthRoots` (and `Polynomial.card_nthRoots`) give the n distinct roots.

annotations.label: Principal n th root

anchor.section: n th roots of a complex number
anchor.snippet: It is usual to choose one of the n n th roots as the principal root

annotations.note: Principal root is `z ^ (1/n : ℂ)` via `Complex.cpow`; no `principalNthRoot` decl.

annotations.label: No continuous n th root on all of C

anchor.section: n th roots of a complex number
anchor.snippet: it is not possible to define a n th root function that is continuous in the whole complex plane

annotations.note: No dedicated obstruction lemma; related discontinuity of `Complex.log` at 0 is known.

anchor.snippet: The n th roots of unity are the

annotations.note: `rootsOfUnity n M` defines the subgroup of nth roots of unity.

annotations.label: Primitive n th roots of unity

anchor.snippet: are called primitive n th roots of unity

annotations.note: `IsPrimitiveRoot ζ n` formalises primitive nth roots of unity.

annotations.label: n th roots via roots of unity

anchor.snippet: The n th roots of unity allow expressing all n th roots of a complex number

annotations.note: Mathlib relates nth roots to primitive roots, e.g. via `IsPrimitiveRoot.nthRoots_eq`.

annotations.label: Geometric arrangement of roots of unity

anchor.snippet: the n th roots of unity lie on the unit circle

annotations.note: Roots of unity arise as `exp (2π i k / n)` via `Complex.isPrimitiveRoot_exp`; no specific 'regular polygon' lemma.

annotations.label: Principal primitive n th root of unity

anchor.snippet: it is called the principal primitive n th root of unity

annotations.note: `Complex.isPrimitiveRoot_exp` shows `exp (2π i / n)` is a primitive root.

annotations.label: Complex exponentiation via logarithm

anchor.snippet: the complex logarithm is used to define complex exponentiation

annotations.note: `Complex.cpow_def` defines `z^w = exp (log z * w)` (with special cases).

annotations.label: Principal value of complex logarithm

anchor.snippet: The principal value of the complex logarithm is the unique continuous function

annotations.note: `Complex.log` is Mathlib's principal-value complex logarithm.

annotations.note: `Complex.cpow` is the principal-branch complex exponentiation.

anchor.snippet: The identity log( b x ) = x ⋅ log b holds whenever b is a positive real number

annotations.label: Failure of (bc)^x = b^x c^x for complex

anchor.section: Failure of power and logarithm identities
anchor.snippet: The identities ( bc ) x = b x c x and ( b / c ) x = b x / c x are valid

annotations.note: Conditional cpow identities exist but the failure statement is not formalised.

anchors.section: Failure of power and logarithm identities
anchors.snippet: The identities ( bc ) x = b x c x and ( b / c ) x = b x / c x are valid

annotations.label: Clausen's paradox

anchor.section: Failure of power and logarithm identities
anchor.snippet: The identity ( e x ) y = e xy holds for real numbers x and y

annotations.note: Clausen's paradox is not stated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Algebraic base, rational exponent gives algebraic

anchor.snippet: If b is a positive real algebraic number , and x is a rational number, then b x is an algebraic number

annotations.note: `IsAlgebraic.pow` covers integer powers; the rational exponent case via nth roots is not given a single Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Gelfond–Schneider theorem

anchor.snippet: Gelfond–Schneider theorem asserts that all values of b x are transcendental

annotations.note: Gelfond–Schneider is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponentiation in a monoid

anchor.snippet: An algebraic structure consisting of a set together with an associative operation denoted multiplicatively

annotations.note: `Monoid.npow` is monoid exponentiation.

annotations.label: Negative integer exponent in a monoid

anchor.snippet: If n is a negative integer

annotations.note: `DivInvMonoid.zpow` extends to negative integer exponents.

annotations.label: Laws of integer exponentiation in algebra

anchor.snippet: Exponentiation with integer exponents obeys the following laws

annotations.note: Integer exponent laws are `zpow_add`, `zpow_mul`, `mul_zpow`, etc.

annotations.label: Multiplicative group

anchor.snippet: A multiplicative group is a set with as associative operation denoted as multiplication

annotations.note: `Group` is the standard Mathlib typeclass.

annotations.label: Powers of a group element form a subgroup

anchor.snippet: The set of all powers of an element of a group form a subgroup

annotations.note: `Subgroup.zpowers x` is the subgroup of integer powers of `x`.

annotations.label: Cyclic group generated by x and order

anchor.snippet: the cyclic group generated by x

annotations.note: `Subgroup.zpowers x` is the cyclic subgroup; `orderOf x` its order.

annotations.label: Order divides group order (Lagrange)

anchor.snippet: the order of an element in a finite group is always a divisor of the number of elements of the group

annotations.note: `orderOf_dvd_card` proves `orderOf x ∣ Fintype.card G`.

annotations.label: Conjugation notation

anchor.snippet: Superscript notation is also used for conjugation

annotations.note: Mathlib has `conj_pow` lemmas but no `x^y` conjugation notation.

annotations.label: Nilpotent element

anchor.snippet: Such an element is said to be nilpotent

annotations.note: `IsNilpotent` is Mathlib's nilpotency predicate.

annotations.label: Nilradical of a commutative ring

anchor.snippet: the nilpotent elements form an ideal , called the nilradical of the ring

annotations.note: `nilradical R` is the ideal of nilpotent elements.

annotations.label: Reduced ring

anchor.snippet: the commutative ring is said to be reduced

annotations.note: `IsReduced R` formalises reduced rings.

annotations.label: Radical of an ideal

anchor.snippet: the set of the elements of R that have a power in I is an ideal, called the radical of I

annotations.note: `Ideal.radical I` is the radical of an ideal.

annotations.label: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz (radical ideals)

anchor.snippet: an ideal is radical if and only if it is the set of all polynomials that are zero on an affine algebraic set

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Nullstellensatz` file proves the radical version.

annotations.label: Matrix power

anchor.snippet: the product of A with itself n times is called the matrix power

annotations.note: Square matrices form a monoid, so `A^n` is given by `Monoid.npow`; `Matrix.zpow` for integer exponents.

annotations.label: Matrix powers in dynamical systems

anchor.snippet: Matrix powers appear often in the context of discrete dynamical systems

annotations.note: Illustrative example; no dedicated Mathlib decl.

annotations.label: Powers of the derivative operator

anchor.snippet: The n th power of the differentiation operator is the n th derivative

annotations.note: `iteratedDeriv n` is the nth iterate of the derivative operator.

annotations.label: Field

anchor.snippet: A field is an algebraic structure in which multiplication, addition, subtraction, and division are defined

annotations.note: `Field` is the standard Mathlib typeclass.

annotations.label: Finite field

anchor.snippet: A finite field is a field with a finite number of elements

annotations.note: Finite fields are realised as `Field` with `Finite`; `GaloisField p n` is the canonical construction.

annotations.label: Primitive element of a finite field

anchor.snippet: A primitive element in

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the unit group is cyclic, implying a primitive element exists; no `primitiveElement` decl specific to finite fields.

annotations.label: Freshman's dream in characteristic p

anchor.snippet: the freshman's dream identity

annotations.note: `add_pow_char` proves `(x+y)^p = x^p + y^p` in characteristic p.

annotations.label: Frobenius automorphism

anchor.snippet: is linear over

annotations.note: `frobenius R p` is the Frobenius ring homomorphism.

annotations.label: Galois group of finite field extension

anchor.snippet: the Galois group of

annotations.note: Mathlib's `GaloisField` and Frobenius theory cover the structure but no single 'Gal = ⟨Frob⟩' decl was found.

annotations.label: Diffie–Hellman key exchange

anchor.snippet: The Diffie–Hellman key exchange is an application of exponentiation in finite fields

annotations.note: Cryptographic application; not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cartesian product of sets

anchor.snippet: The Cartesian product of two sets S and T is the set of the ordered pairs

annotations.note: `Set.prod` is the Cartesian product of sets.

annotations.label: n th power of a set

anchor.snippet: This allows defining the n th power

annotations.note: Iterated Cartesian power is realised via `Fin n → S` or `Set.pi`, but there is no `Set.npow` decl.

annotations.label: Set of functions notation S^T

anchor.snippet: the set of all functions from T to S is denoted

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `T → S` (the Pi type) for function sets; cardinal exponentiation `#S ^ #T` matches by `Cardinal.power_def`.

annotations.label: Canonical isomorphisms (currying)

anchor.snippet: This exponential notation is justified by the following canonical isomorphisms

annotations.note: `Equiv.curry` gives `(α × β → γ) ≃ (α → β → γ)`.

annotations.label: Power set as 2^S

anchor.snippet: denotes the power set of S

annotations.note: `Set.powerset` and `Finset.powerset` realise `2^S`; cardinality via `Fintype.card_finset`.

annotations.label: Cardinal exponentiation

anchor.snippet: This fits in with the exponentiation of cardinal numbers

annotations.note: `Cardinal.power_def` gives `#α ^ #β = #(β → α)`.

annotations.label: Exponentiation as right adjoint to product

anchor.snippet: the functor "exponentiation to the power T " is a right adjoint to the functor "direct product with T "

annotations.note: `CategoryTheory.exp.adjunction` is the prod ⊣ exp adjunction in Cartesian closed categories.

annotations.label: Cartesian closed category

anchor.snippet: A category is called a Cartesian closed category

annotations.note: `CartesianClosed` is Mathlib's typeclass for CCCs.

annotations.label: Tetration and hyperoperation

anchor.snippet: this operation is sometimes called hyper-4 or tetration

annotations.note: `hyperoperation` defines the hyperoperation hierarchy with tetration at level 4.

annotations.label: Hyperoperation evaluated at (3,3)

anchor.snippet: Evaluated at (3, 3)

annotations.note: Specific numerical evaluation example; not a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Limits of x^y on extended reals

anchor.snippet: f has a limit at all accumulation points of D

annotations.note: Mathlib has continuity of `rpow` and various limit lemmas but no single 'limit existence on D' decl.

annotations.label: Values of limits of x^y at boundary cases

anchor.snippet: Under this definition by continuity, we obtain

annotations.note: Specific boundary-value table is not packaged as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Function composition

anchor.snippet: Function composition is a binary operation that is defined on functions

annotations.note: `Function.comp` is composition; `∘` notation.

annotations.label: n th iterate of a function

anchor.snippet: this defines the n th power of the function under composition

annotations.note: `Function.iterate f n` is the nth iterate `f^[n]`.

annotations.label: Inverse function notation f^{-1}

anchor.snippet: denotes always the inverse function

annotations.note: `Function.invFun` / `Equiv.symm` formalise the inverse-function notation.
